title: LGBTQ literature
articleText:
== Summary ==
LGBTQ literature, sometimes called queer literature, refers to works with LGBTQ themes, characters, or authors. It is a subset of LGBTQ media. There are LGBTQ literary awards to recognize works in this tradition.
LGBTQ literature may refer to:

Works by LGBTQ authors
Works centering LGBTQ characters, including in modern written fiction and graphic arts
Other literature featuring the LGBTQ community
Subtypes of LGBTQ literature include:

Lesbian literature
Gay literature
Bisexual literature
Transgender literature
Intersex literature

== By country ==
LGBTQ literature in Argentina
LGBTQ literature in Australia
LGBTQ literature in Brazil
China: Danmei
LGBTQ literature in Canada
LGBTQ literature in Chile
LGBTQ literature in Colombia
LGBTQ literature in Costa Rica
LGBTQ literature in Ecuador
LGBTQ literature in El Salvador
LGBTQ literature in France
LGBTQ literature in Guatemala
LGBTQ literature in Iceland
India: LGBTQ literature in India, Tamil literary works on sexual minorities
Japan: Boys' Love, Gay Manga, Yuri
LGBTQ literature in Mexico
Netherlands and Flanders: LGBTQ literature in the Dutch-language area
LGBTQ literature in New Zealand
LGBTQ literature in Singapore
LGBTQ literature in Spain
LGBTQ literature in Sri Lanka
Taiwan: Tongzhi literature
United States: Black lesbian literature in the United States, LGBTQ Latino literature
LGBTQ literature in Venezuela

== By genre or style ==
LGBTQ periodicals
LGBTQ romance
LGBTQ-themed speculative fiction
LGBTQ themes in comics
LGBTQ themes in horror fiction
LGBTQ themes in mythology
Timeline of LGBTQ journalism
Further subgenres include:

Gay pulp fiction
Gay teen fiction
Homoerotic poetry
Lesbian periodicals
Lesbian pulp fiction
Slash fiction
Trans poetry

== Related topics ==
Category:LGBTQ book publishing companies
Category:LGBTQ museums and archives
LGBTQ bookstores
LGBTQ literary awards
Libraries and the LGBTQ community

facts.text: LGBTQ literature, sometimes called queer literature, refers to works with LGBTQ themes, characters, or authors.
LGBTQ literature is a subset of LGBTQ media.
There are LGBTQ literary awards specifically to recognize works in the LGBTQ literary tradition.
Subtypes of LGBTQ literature include lesbian literature, gay literature, bisexual literature, transgender literature, and intersex literature.
Gay pulp fiction and lesbian pulp fiction are subgenres of LGBTQ literature.
Slash fiction is a recognized subgenre within LGBTQ literature.
Japan has distinct LGBTQ literary subgenres including Boys Love (BL), Gay Manga, and Yuri.
China has a distinct LGBTQ literary tradition called Danmei.
Taiwan has a LGBTQ literary tradition called Tongzhi literature.
Lesbian literature is a subgenre of literature addressing lesbian themes, including poetry, plays, fiction, and non-fiction.
A similar term to lesbian literature is sapphic literature, which encompasses works featuring love between women not necessarily defined as lesbian.
The fundamental work of lesbian literature is considered to be the poetry of Sappho of Lesbos.
Sappho of Lesbos wrote about women's daily lives, their relationships, rituals, and the beauty of women.
Samuel Taylor Coleridge published his unfinished narrative poem 'Christabel' at the beginning of the 19th century, which scholars have interpreted as having lesbian implications.
In 1866, Algernon Charles Swinburne published Poems and Ballads, which contained poems 'Anactoria' and 'Sapphics' dealing explicitly with lesbian content.
Henry James portrayed a Boston marriage — considered an early form of lesbian relationship — in his 1886 novel The Bostonians.
Marian Halcombe in Wilkie Collins's 1859 novel The Woman in White has been interpreted by scholars as having lesbian potential.
Historically, the term 'gay literature' was often used to cover both gay male and lesbian literature.
Contemporary lesbian literature has historically been centered around several small, exclusively lesbian presses as well as online fandoms.
The Lambda Literary Award for Lesbian Fiction is a notable award recognizing works of lesbian literature.
LGBTQ-themed speculative fiction and LGBTQ themes in comics are recognized subgenres within broader LGBTQ literature.
LGBTQ romance as a genre has met with increasing acceptance and sales from the 1980s onward.
As of 2020, presses specializing in LGBTQ romance, mainstream publishers, and booksellers are expanding their LGBTQ offerings and inviting more diverse authorship.
Gay teen fiction is a recognized subgenre within LGBTQ literature.
Homoerotic poetry and trans poetry are recognized subgenres within LGBTQ literature.

title: Paranormal Romance
articleText:
== Summary ==
Paranormal romance is a subgenre of both romantic fiction and speculative fiction. Paranormal romance focuses on romantic love and includes elements beyond the range of scientific explanation, from the speculative fiction genres of fantasy, science fiction, and horror. Paranormal romance range from traditional romances with a paranormal setting to stories with a science fiction or fantasy-based plot with a romantic subplot included. Romantic relationships between humans and vampires, shapeshifters, ghosts, and other entities of a fantastic or otherworldly nature are common. 
Beyond the more prevalent themes involving vampires, shapeshifters, ghosts, or time travel, paranormal romances can also include books featuring characters with psychic abilities, such as telekinesis or telepathy. Paranormal romance's most recent revival has been spurred by turn of the 21st century technology; for example, the internet and electronic publishing. Paranormal romances are one of the fastest-growing trends in the romance genre.
Examples of authors specializing in this genre include Dani Harper, Nalini Singh, Jessica Bird, Kresley Cole, Christine Feehan, Kelley Armstrong, and Stephenie Meyer, [...]

== Definition and subgenres ==
Paranormal romance blends the real with the fantastic or science fictional. The fantastic elements may be woven into an alternate version of our own world in an urban fantasy involving vampires, demons, and/or werewolves, or they may be more "normal" manifestations of the paranormal—humans with psychic abilities, witches, or ghosts. Time travel, futuristic, and extraterrestrial romances also fall beneath the paranormal umbrella.
These novels often blend elements of other subgenres, including suspense and mystery, with their fantastic themes. A few paranormals are set solely in the past and are structured much like any historical romance novel. Others are set in the future, sometimes on different worlds. Still others have a time-travel element with either the hero or the heroine traveling into the past or the future. Between 2002 and 2004, the number of paranormal romances published in the United States doubled to 170 per year. A popular title in the genre can sell over 500,000 copies.
As in the fantasy subgenre known as urban fantasy, many paranormal romances rely on the blend of contemporary life with the existence of supernatural or magically empowered beings, human or [...]

== See also ==

Magical girlfriend
Monster erotica


== References ==

facts.text: Paranormal romance is a subgenre of both romantic fiction and speculative fiction.
Paranormal romance focuses on romantic love and includes elements beyond the range of scientific explanation, drawing from fantasy, science fiction, and horror genres.
Romantic relationships between humans and vampires, shapeshifters, ghosts, and other fantastical entities are common tropes in paranormal romance.
Paranormal romance can also feature characters with psychic abilities such as telekinesis or telepathy.
Paranormal romance's most recent revival has been spurred by turn-of-the-21st-century technology such as the internet and electronic publishing.
Paranormal romances are described as one of the fastest-growing trends in the romance genre.
Between 2002 and 2004, the number of paranormal romances published in the United States doubled to 170 per year.
A popular paranormal romance title can sell over 500,000 copies.
Authors specializing in paranormal romance include Nalini Singh, Christine Feehan, Kelley Armstrong, and Stephenie Meyer.
Stephenie Meyer is an author noted for specializing in the paranormal romance genre.
Paranormal romance blends the real with the fantastic or science fictional, often set in an alternate version of our world involving vampires, demons, and/or werewolves.
Time travel, futuristic, and extraterrestrial romances all fall beneath the paranormal romance umbrella.
Paranormal romance novels often blend elements of suspense and mystery with their fantastic themes.
Many paranormal romance novels rely on the blend of contemporary life with the existence of supernatural or magically empowered beings, similar to the urban fantasy subgenre.
Some paranormal romances are set solely in the past and are structured much like historical romance novels.
Paranormal romance ranges from traditional romances with a paranormal setting to stories with a science fiction or fantasy-based plot with a romantic subplot.
Kresley Cole and Christine Feehan are noted authors specializing in the paranormal romance genre.
Monster erotica is a related subgenre listed alongside paranormal romance in literary classifications.
The paranormal romance genre grew substantially in the early 2000s, with the number of U.S. titles published doubling in just two years.
Urban fantasy — involving supernatural beings in contemporary city settings — is a key subgenre related to paranormal romance.

title: Romance novel
articleText:
== Summary ==
A romance or romantic novel is a genre fiction work focused on the relationship and romantic love between two people, often concluding with an emotionally satisfying or optimistic ending. Authors who have significantly contributed to the development of this genre include Samuel Richardson, Frances Burney, Maria Edgeworth, Jane Austen, Charlotte Brontë, Emily Brontë, and Anne Brontë.
Romance novels can encompass various subgenres, such as bodice ripper, fantasy, contemporary, historical romance, paranormal fiction, sapphic, and science fiction. They may also contain tropes such as enemies to lovers, second chance, and forced proximity. While women have traditionally been considered the primary readers of romance novels, a 2017 study commissioned by the Romance Writers of America found that men accounted for 18% of romance book buyers.
The genre of works conventionally referred to as "romance novels" existed in ancient Greece. Precursors can also be found in the literary fiction of the 18th and 19th centuries, including Samuel Richardson's sentimental novel Pamela, or Virtue Rewarded (1740) and the novels of Jane Austen. Austen inspired Georgette Heyer, the British author of [...]

== Definition ==
Women will pick up a romance novel knowing what to expect, and this foreknowledge of the reader is very important. When the hero and heroine meet and fall in love, maybe they don't know they're in love but the reader does. Then a conflict will draw them apart, but you know in the end they'll be back together, and preferably married or planning to be by page 192.
According to the Romance Writers of America, the main plot of a mass-market romance novel must revolve about the two people as they develop romantic love for each other and work to build a relationship. Both the conflict and the climax of the novel should be directly related to that core theme of developing a romantic relationship, although the novel can also contain subplots that do not specifically relate to the main characters' romantic love. Furthermore, a romance novel must have an "emotionally satisfying and optimistic ending."
Leslie Gelbman, a president of Berkley Books, defines the genre as stating that romance must make the "romantic relationship between the hero and the heroine ... the core of the book." In general, romance novels reward characters who are good people and penalize those who are evil, and a [...]

== History ==


== Critical reception ==
Some important literary figures received critical acclaim for their romance novels. For instance, the Nobel laureate Gabriel García Márquez received critical praise for his romance novel Love in the Time of Cholera.
Arthur Schopenhauer held that, if poets and novelists across continents have not stopped producing romance novels since millennia, it is because no other topic is more relevant and so the theme never gets old. According to Schopenhauer, romance/love is more important than other topics because it affects the species (not only the individual), in the sense that romance and partner choice is generally a prerequisite to have offspring and continue maintaining the human species in future generations of humankind.
Despite recent rehabilitation and merging of the genre with other genres, there is sometimes a negative stigma with the romance novel. As such, some dedicated readers are embarrassed to admit to buying or even reading the books. Some critics point to a lack of suspense, as it may seem obvious that the hero and heroine will eventually resolve their issues, and wonder whether it is beneficial "for women to be whiling away so many hours reading impossibly glamorized [...]

== Formats ==
Romance novels are divided into two sub-sets, category romances, also known as series romances, and single title romances. Many authors write only within one of the formats, but others, including Jennifer Crusie and Jayne Ann Krentz, have achieved success in both formats.

== Subgenres ==
Because the [...]

facts.text: A romance novel is a genre fiction work focused on the romantic love between two people, often concluding with an emotionally satisfying or optimistic ending.
Authors who significantly contributed to the development of the romance novel genre include Samuel Richardson, Frances Burney, Maria Edgeworth, Jane Austen, and the Brontë sisters.
Romance novels can encompass subgenres such as bodice ripper, fantasy, contemporary, historical romance, paranormal fiction, sapphic, and science fiction.
Common romance novel tropes include enemies to lovers, second chance, and forced proximity.
A 2017 study commissioned by the Romance Writers of America found that men accounted for 18% of romance book buyers.
The genre of works referred to as romance novels existed in ancient Greece.
Samuel Richardson's sentimental novel Pamela, or Virtue Rewarded (1740) is considered a key precursor to the modern romance novel.
Jane Austen inspired Georgette Heyer, the British author considered a foundational figure in the historical romance subgenre.
According to the Romance Writers of America, a romance novel must have an 'emotionally satisfying and optimistic ending.'
According to the Romance Writers of America definition, the main plot of a mass-market romance novel must revolve around two people developing romantic love for each other.
Leslie Gelbman, a president of Berkley Books, defines romance as requiring the romantic relationship between the hero and heroine to be the core of the book.
In general, romance novels reward characters who are good people and penalize those who are evil.
Nobel laureate Gabriel García Márquez received critical praise for his romance novel Love in the Time of Cholera.
Arthur Schopenhauer argued that romance novels remain relevant because love affects the species, not just individuals, being a prerequisite for having offspring.
There is sometimes a negative stigma attached to romance novels, with some dedicated readers embarrassed to admit buying or reading them.
Romance novels are divided into two sub-sets: category romances (also called series romances) and single title romances.
Authors Jennifer Crusie and Jayne Ann Krentz have achieved success in both category romance and single title romance formats.
The bodice ripper is a recognized subgenre of romance novels.
Sapphic romance — featuring romantic love between women — is a recognized subgenre of romance novels.
Critics of romance novels point to a perceived lack of suspense, as it often seems obvious the hero and heroine will eventually resolve their issues.
Forced proximity is a recognized romance novel trope where characters are brought into close physical or situational contact.
Both the conflict and the climax of a romance novel should be directly related to the core theme of developing a romantic relationship, per the Romance Writers of America.

title: Self-publishing
articleText:
== Summary ==
Self-publishing is an author-driven publication of any media without the involvement of a third-party publisher. Since the advent of the internet, self-publishing usually depends on digital platforms and print-on-demand technology, ranging from physical books to eBooks. Examples include magazines, print-on-demand books, music albums, pamphlets, brochures, video games, video content, artwork, zines, and web fiction. Self-publishing, therefore, emerges as an alternative to traditional publishing, with implications for production, cost and revenue, distribution, and public perception.

== Types ==
In self-publishing authors publish their own work.  While it is possible for an author to single-handedly carry out the whole process independently, many authors engage with professionals for specific services as needed (such as editors or cover designers). A growing number of companies offer a one-stop shop where an author can source a whole range of services required to self-publish a book (sometimes called "Assisted Self-publishing Providers" or "Self-publishing Service Providers").
Other forms of publishing include

hybrid publishing, (where the publisher and author collaborate and share costs and risks. In return, the author may be required to surrender some control and/or rights in return for the publisher's financial and other contribution) OR
vanity publishing, (where the author pays for the cost of all services, but also signs a restrictive contract which usually involves surrendering significant rights).

== History ==


== Production and control ==
Self-publishing provides authors with autonomy over both the production process and content and are not required to make changes based on feedback or market changes. Authors also determine pricing and can change it at any point in time. In traditional publishing, these aspects are subject to negotiations and collaboration.
Self-publishing also offers an expedited timeline where authors can release their work within a few weeks after their work is finished by bypassing extra time spent on looking for an agent, publisher, and editor.
While self-publishing offers greater control and speed, it places production responsibility on the author, which includes editing, cover design, formatting, and marketing. These tasks may be time-consuming and require specialized skills to complete, which may necessitate hiring freelancers. Traditional publishers typically cover these services as part of their contract with the author.

== Cost and revenue ==
Self-publishing's financial aspect differs from traditional models. In self-publishing, authors are responsible for all associated costs, which may include editing, cover design, formatting, and marketing. Self-published authors receive a four to five times a larger payout per sale with royalties sometimes as high as 70% when compared to working with traditional publishers. However, total earnings depend on sales volume, which can be influenced by discoverability. Traditional publishers often provide an advance that is later covered by author's earnings as their work sells. Self-published authors do not receive an advance. The financial success of self-published authors depends on the author's marketing efforts, which often require a substantial investment.

== Distribution and reach ==
Self-published authors often face challenges in achieving wide distribution, particularly in physical bookstores and libraries. Traditional publishers have established relationships with bookstores and libraries, often because of distribution networks and contracts that may be difficult for a self-publisher to replicate. Self-published authors often have more success when utilizing online retailers and print-on-demand services, which allows them to reach readers but at a limited physical presence.

== Publishing platforms ==


== Self-published bestsellers ==
While most self-published books do not make much money, there are self-published authors who [...]

facts.text: Self-publishing is an author-driven publication of any media without the involvement of a third-party publisher.
Since the advent of the internet, self-publishing usually depends on digital platforms and print-on-demand technology.
Self-publishing covers a broad range of media including magazines, print-on-demand books, music albums, pamphlets, video games, video content, artwork, zines, and web fiction.
Self-publishing emerges as an alternative to traditional publishing with implications for production, cost, revenue, distribution, and public perception.
Hybrid publishing is a model where the publisher and author collaborate and share costs and risks, with the author potentially surrendering some control and/or rights.
Vanity publishing is a model where the author pays for the cost of all services but signs a restrictive contract usually involving surrendering significant rights.
Self-publishing provides authors with autonomy over both the production process and content, without being required to make changes based on feedback or market changes.
Self-publishing authors can determine their own pricing and change it at any point in time.
Self-publishing offers an expedited timeline where authors can release their work within a few weeks after finishing.
Self-published authors receive a four to five times larger payout per sale compared to traditional publishing, with royalties sometimes as high as 70%.
Traditional publishers provide authors with an advance payment that is later covered by the author's earnings as their work sells; self-published authors do not receive an advance.
Self-published authors often face challenges in achieving wide distribution in physical bookstores and libraries.
Self-published authors tend to have more success using online retailers and print-on-demand services rather than physical bookstores.
Traditional publishers have established relationships with bookstores and libraries through distribution networks that are difficult for self-publishers to replicate.
While self-publishing offers greater control and speed, it places production responsibility on the author, including editing, cover design, formatting, and marketing.
Some self-publishing companies offer an all-in-one service sometimes called 'Assisted Self-publishing Providers' or 'Self-publishing Service Providers.'
While most self-published books do not make much money, some self-published authors have achieved significant commercial success.
The financial success of self-published authors depends heavily on the author's marketing efforts, which often require a substantial investment.
Self-published authors' total earnings depend on sales volume, which can be influenced by discoverability on retail platforms.
Print-on-demand technology is a key enabler of modern self-publishing, allowing books to be printed as they are ordered rather than in bulk.
eBooks and digital platforms have expanded the reach of self-published works, allowing authors to distribute globally without physical inventory.
In traditional publishing, aspects such as pricing, content changes, and production are subject to negotiations and collaboration between author and publisher.
